title: Watch 434 Avant-Garde and Surreal Short Films Online: Salvador DalГӯ, Marcel Duchamp, Luis BuГұuel and Many More
description: Much has been writВӯten lateВӯly about the criВӯsis in HolВӯlyВӯwood, which has left many apparВӯentВӯly sure-fire blockВӯbusters flounВӯderВӯing, theВӯater
body:
Much has been writВӯten lateВӯly about the criВӯsis in HolВӯlyВӯwood, which has left many apparВӯentВӯly sure-fire blockВӯbusters flounВӯderВӯing, theВӯaters empВӯty, and proВӯducВӯtion jobs lost. There are many facВӯtors in play вҖ” some of them, as few diagВӯnoses fail to point out, strucВӯturВӯal вҖ” but can we ignore the posВӯsiВӯbilВӯiВӯty of fatigue, perВӯhaps even boreВӯdom, with film itself? WeвҖҷve postВӯed in recent years here on Open CulВӯture about the decay of cinВӯeВӯma, the rise of вҖңvisuВӯal muzakвҖқ on NetВӯflix,В why movies donвҖҷt feel real anyВӯmore, andВ why movies donвҖҷt even feel like movies anyВӯmore. Even if theyвҖҷve limВӯitВӯed their expoВӯsure to big-budВӯget specВӯtaВӯcles, most once-avid cinephiles will have felt all those pheВӯnomВӯeВӯna for themВӯselves by now, and many will be conВӯsidВӯerВӯing whether to look for a new art form to enjoy. But some will wonВӯder: maybe thereвҖҷs a cure?


	

There could well be, and a bracВӯing one. If you seek a re-enchantВӯment with film, there could be few betВӯter places to look than in the work of filmВӯmakВӯers who have broВӯken that mediВӯum down to its very comВӯpoВӯnents and put it togethВӯer again in unconВӯvenВӯtionВӯal ways. Some of the results shocked audiВӯences fifty, sixВӯty, sevВӯenВӯty, even a hunВӯdred years ago вҖ” and indeed, some retain that powВӯer today.
You can take a jourВӯney through the hisВӯtoВӯry of such experВӯiВӯmenВӯtal, avant-garde, and surВӯreВӯal motion picВӯtures with the YouTube playlist at the top of the post, which comВӯprisВӯes 434 such videos. The exact numВӯber will vary dependВӯing upon your region of the world, as well as upon how many of them have come and gone since the playlistвҖҷs creВӯation. WhatВӯevВӯer the total, not even a fringe-cinВӯeВӯma habituГ© will have seen everyВӯthing on it (at least, not more than once).


	

LongВӯtime Open CulВӯture readВӯers may recВӯogВӯnize on the playlist the work of Dadaist Hans Richter and MarВӯcel Duchamp, abstracВӯtion pioВӯneer Viking Eggeling, earВӯly femВӯiВӯnist filmВӯmakВӯer GerВӯmaine Dulac, and aniВӯmaВӯtor (as well as city symВӯphonВӯist) WalВӯter Ruttmann, not to menВӯtion SalВӯvador DalГӯ and Luis BuГұuel.


	

They may or may not already have encounВӯtered the cinВӯeВӯmatВӯic legaВӯcy of, say, ShЕ«ji TerВӯayaВӯma, the all-around avant-gardist and provoВӯcaВӯteur whose influВӯence is still felt in JapanВӯese art today; Stan Brakhage, who forewent even the use of a camВӯera and creВӯatВӯed his own cinВӯeВӯma by manipВӯuВӯlatВӯing film directВӯly; or Michael Snow, whose WaveВӯlength tells a stoВӯry withВӯout leavВӯing a sinВӯgle room in which very litВӯtle hapВӯpens. But then, after enough of these experВӯiВӯmenВӯtal, avant-garde, and surВӯreВӯal viewВӯing expeВӯriВӯences, youвҖҷll rememВӯber that there are many ways for a film to tell a stoВӯry вҖ” and much, much more that film can do besides stoВӯryВӯtelling, if only weвҖҷd let it.


	

RelatВӯed ConВӯtent:
A Page of MadВӯness: The Lost Avant Garde MasВӯterВӯpiece from EarВӯly JapanВӯese CinВӯeВӯma (1926)
The EvocaВӯtiveВӯness of DecomВӯposВӯing Film: Watch the 1926 HolВӯlyВӯwood Movie The Bells Become the ExperВӯiВӯmenВӯtal 2004 Short Film Light Is CallВӯing
Watch the MedВӯiВӯtaВӯtive CinepoВӯem вҖңH20вҖқ: A LandВӯmark Avant-Garde Art Film from 1929
Watch 3000 Years of Art, a 1968 ExperВӯiВӯmenВӯtal Film That Takes You on a VisuВӯal JourВӯney Through 3,000 Years of Fine Art
Watch MeshВӯes of the AfterВӯnoon, the ExperВӯiВӯmenВӯtal Short VotВӯed the 16th Best Film of All Time
Paul SchradВӯer CreВӯates a DiaВӯgram MapВӯping the ProВӯgresВӯsion of ArtВӯhouse CinВӯeВӯma: Ozu, BresВӯson, Tarkovsky & OthВӯer Auteurs
Based in Seoul,В ColВӯinВ MarshallВ writes and broadВӯcasts on cities, lanВӯguage, and culВӯture. HeвҖҷs the author of the newsletВӯterВ Books on CitiesВ as well as the booksВ н•ңкөӯ мҡ”м•Ҫ кёҲм§ҖВ (No SumВӯmaВӯrizВӯing Korea) andВ KoreВӯan Newtro.В FolВӯlow him on the social netВӯwork forВӯmerВӯly known as TwitВӯter atВ @colinmarshall.

title: How a Clean, Tidy Home Can Help You Survive the Atomic Bomb: A Cold War Film from 1954
description:
in History |   	April 6th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	


Not too far back, we revisВӯitВӯed some Cold War proВӯpaВӯganВӯda that taught upstandВӯing Ame
body:
in History |   	April 6th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	


Not too far back, we revisВӯitВӯed some Cold War proВӯpaВӯganВӯda that taught upstandВӯing AmerВӯiВӯcan citВӯiВӯzens How to Spot a ComВӯmuВӯnist Using LitВӯerВӯary CritВӯiВӯcism. ItвҖҷs a gem, but it has nothВӯing on the 1954 film,В The House in the MidВӯdle. SelectВӯedВ for preserВӯvaВӯtion in theВ NationВӯal Film RegВӯistry by the Library of ConВӯgress, the short docВӯuВӯmenВӯtary makes the ultiВӯmate case for cleanВӯliВӯness. BringВӯing viewВӯers to the NevaВӯda ProvВӯing Grounds, the 12-minute film shows what hapВӯpens when clean, white housВӯes are subВӯjectВӯed to heat waves from an atomВӯic blast, verВӯsus what hapВӯpens when a dingy, ill-kept house goes through the same drill. It turns out that neat peoВӯple can not only claim moral vicВӯtoВӯry (as they always do). They also get to live anothВӯer day. ConВӯsidВӯer it proof of the surВӯvival of the tidiВӯest.
The film was proВӯduced byВ the NationВӯal Clean Up-Paint Up-Fix Up Bureau with supВӯport fromВ theВ FedВӯerВӯal CivВӯil Defense AdminВӯisВӯtraВӯtion. TheВ NationВӯal Paint, VarВӯnish and LacВӯquer AssoВӯciВӯaВӯtion also apparВӯentВӯly played a role, sugВӯgestВӯing that corВӯpoВӯrate interВӯests were capВӯiВӯtalВӯizВӯing on wartime fear. Not the first time thatвҖҷs hapВӯpened in AmerВӯiВӯca. Or that lastвҖҰ
If you would like to sign up for Open CultureвҖҷs free email newsletВӯter,В please find it here. ItвҖҷs a great way to see our new posts, all bunВӯdled in one email, each day.
If you would like to supВӯport the misВӯsion of Open CulВӯture, conВӯsidВӯer makВӯingВ a donaВӯtion to our site. ItвҖҷs hard to rely 100% on ads, and your conВӯtriВӯbuВӯtions will help us conВӯtinВӯue proВӯvidВӯing the best free culВӯturВӯal and eduВӯcaВӯtionВӯal mateВӯriВӯals to learnВӯers everyВӯwhere. You can conВӯtribute through PayВӯPal, PatreВӯon, and VenВӯmo (@openculture). Thanks!
Note: An earВӯliВӯer verВӯsion of this post appeared on our site in 2013.
RelatВӯed ConВӯtent:
Watch Ducked and CovВӯered: A SurВӯvival Guide to the Post ApocВӯaВӯlypse (A LitВӯtle NSFW)
How to Spot a ComВӯmuВӯnistВ by Using LitВӯerВӯary CritВӯiВӯcism: A 1955 ManВӯuВӯal from the U.S. MilВӯiВӯtary
AniВӯmatВӯed Films Made DurВӯing the Cold War Explain Why AmerВӯiВӯca is ExcepВӯtionВӯalВӯly ExcepВӯtionВӯal
The Red MenВӯace: A StrikВӯing Gallery of Anti-ComВӯmuВӯnist Posters, Ads, ComВӯic Books, MagВӯaВӯzines & Films
Watch вҖңDonвҖҷt Be a SuckВӯer!,вҖқ the 1947 US GovВӯernВӯment Anti-Hatred Film ThatвҖҷs RelВӯeВӯvant All Over Again
How DisВӯney Fought FasВӯcism with ProВӯpaВӯganВӯda CarВӯtoons DurВӯing World War II & AvertВӯed FinanВӯcial ColВӯlapse

title: 12,000 Years Ago, Native Americans Were Playing Games of Chance with Handmade Dice
description: Archaeologists have long known that the ancient peoples of North AmericaвҖ”not unlike usвҖ”played a lot of games. Going back millennia, cultures aroun
body:
Archaeologists have long known that the ancient peoples of North AmericaвҖ”not unlike usвҖ”played a lot of games. Going back millennia, cultures around the world developed myriad ways to keep entertained, and for a long time, it was thought that the first dice ever used could be traced to the ancient Eastern European and Near East cultures of Mesopotamia, the Indus Valley, and the Caucasus. But according to a new paper by Robert Madden, published by Cambridge University Press, games of chance developed much, much earlier than originally thoughtвҖ”halfway around the world.
Researchers previously believed that the earliest dice originated about 5,500 years ago, but Madden shares that examples excavated in North America date back as far as the Late PleistoceneвҖ”the Ice Age. Among the oldest reported examples are a few found in modern-day Wyoming, Colorado, and New Mexico. The rich archaeological sites in these places are associated with the Folsom Culture, representing a dispersed hunter-gatherer lifeway that extended across the North American West, Southwest, and Great Plains around 12,000 years ago.
Examples of dice with details showing microscopic traces of pigment, with color enhanced for illustration
вҖңThe dice tend to show up in liminal spaces where you have a lot of high mobility,вҖқ Madden told Live Science. вҖңIt might have something to do with how separated these people are and the need to relate to people you donвҖҷt see very often.вҖқ
In the report, Madden also says that вҖңthe making and using of dice represent humansвҖҷ first known efforts to intentionally generate, observe, and record streams of controlled, random eventsвҖҰвҖқ He adds that, possibly for the first time, people were comprehending patterns or regularities in probabilityвҖ”a kind of precursor to understanding what we now call the law of large numbers. Anthropologists consider this to be вҖңa crucial early step in humanityвҖҷs evolving discovery and understanding of randomness and the probabilistic nature of the universe.вҖқ
Madden compared hundreds of examples found across the American West with a comprehensive, several-hundred-page publication called Games of the North American Indians, published in 1907 as part of an annual report by the Bureau of American Ethnology. ItвҖҷs currently available in a two-volume edition from Bison Books.
You might also enjoy seeing what may be the worldвҖҷs oldest crayon.
Illustrations of bone dice from Stewart CulinвҖҷs book вҖҳGames of the North American Indians (1907)
Examples of early Native American dice

schema.headline: 12,000 Years Ago, Native Americans Were Playing Games of Chance with Handmade Dice

title: Hillary Waters Fayle Creates 'Portraits of Place' from Seeds, Foliage, and Petals
description: When we think of somewhere weвҖҷve been, what are the first things to come to mind? Perhaps there are memorable smells, a sense of other people bein
body:
When we think of somewhere weвҖҷve been, what are the first things to come to mind? Perhaps there are memorable smells, a sense of other people being around, or a particular quality of light. But what if we remembered landscapes and experiences through plants? For Hillary Waters Fayle, flower petals, seeds, and foliage combine into a kind of album of various places, which she then uses to create bold cyanotypes.
The artist has long worked with botanicals and other organic materials, notably embroidering foraged leaves and feathers with meticulous geometric designs. With the series Portraits of Place, which sheвҖҷs been pursuing for the past six years, Fayle precisely arranges individual petals and leaves into intricate, symmetrical, mandala-like compositions on acrylic.

She starts by collecting and drying botanicals from specific locations, such as Grace Farms Foundation in New Canaan, Connecticut, or Maymont Park in Richmond, Virginia. These are then laid onto watercolor paper thatвҖҷs been painted with UV-sensitive iron salts. After being left out in the sun, these result in the bright blue cyanotypes that chronicle the outlinesвҖ”perhaps one could even say the spiritвҖ”of the distinctive layouts.
вҖңThe way these portraits illustrate a very particular place and time via botany can be a way to define the relationship that the people of that place have with the landвҖ”almost like a modern-day florilegium,вҖқ Fayle tells Colossal. She continues:

All of these pieces are so different and special to me, but it has been particularly meaningful to make portraits of areas that are going to change drastically in the near future, either from development or rising sea levels, fire, etc. It feels like a way of preserving and honoring the land and all that is present there right now.В 

Flowers and plants symbolize the natural evolution of particular spaces, such as a gardener adding new bulbs or birds depositing seeds. They symbolize the nature of seasons and life cycles, emphasizing a relationship that is simultaneously enduring and ephemeral. See more on the artistвҖҷs Instagram.
Chisman Creek Park, Tidewater, Virginia (detail)

schema.headline: Hillary Waters Fayle Creates 'Portraits of Place' from Seeds, Foliage, and Petals

title: 7 Artists We're Excited to See at EXPO CHICAGO 2026
description: From April 9 to 12, EXPO CHICAGO returns to Navy Pier, hosting hundreds of galleries, site-specific projects, talks, and multi-disciplinary progra
body:
From April 9 to 12, EXPO CHICAGO returns to Navy Pier, hosting hundreds of galleries, site-specific projects, talks, and multi-disciplinary programming both downtown and across the city. This week is one of the most exciting times for the Chicago-area art scene, and weвҖҷre excited to share our annual preview of what weвҖҷre most looking forward to!
Aliza Nisenbaum, вҖңHitomiвҖқ (2022), oil on linen, 66 x 57 inches
1. Aliza Nisenbaum at Anton Kern and Regan Projects
Presented by Anton Kern and Regan Projects, Aliza NisenbaumвҖҷs vibrant portraiture portrays her subjects in bold chromatics. NisenbaumвҖҷs smaller-scale works presented at the fair echo one of her larger projects: a celebratory mural titled вҖңReading Circles/ Weaving Dreams/ Seeding FuturesвҖқВ created for the Obama Presidential Center.
Tawny Chatmon, вҖңThe Restoration / Made Whole AgainвҖқ (2024-2025), embroidery and handstitched threadwork on archival pigment print, 30 x 25 inches. Image courtesy of the artist and Galerie Myrtis
2. Tawny Chatmon at Galerie Myrtis
WeвҖҷre always excited for the opportunity to see Tawny ChatmonвҖҷs portraits up close. Galerie Myrtis will be presenting some of the artistвҖҷs newer works, which continue her interest in melding craft techniques with photography. Rather than gold leaf, though, Chatmon embroiders various motifs onto her portraits.
Maya Fuji, вҖң1PM: CleanВ·йҫҚзҘһгҒЁжҷҙгӮҢеҘівҖқ (2026), acrylic and glitter on canvas, 48 x 60 x 1.5 inches
3. Maya Fuji at Charlie James
Referencing a genre of manga popular in the mid-2000s, Maya FujiвҖҷs Slice of Life peers into the everyday activities of her young characters. Lounging, petting a napping cat, and getting ready for the evening take center stage in these vibrant yet calm paintings.
LaKela Brown, вҖңTwo Overlapping Collard LeavesвҖқ (2026), polyurethane and acrylic, 23 Г— 8.5 Г— 2 inches
4. LaKela Brown at 56 Henry
LaKela Brown has traded in her stark white paint for another monochromatic palette, coating her polyurethane sculptures in black. For her solo presentation with 56 Henry, the artist explores ethnobotany and Black life in America through renditions of collard greens, tobacco, corn, chicken wings, and more.
Gerard Mas, вҖңParty Horn LadyвҖқ (2026), polychrome resin; 53 x 36.5 x 26 centimeters, edition 5 of 7
5. Gerard Mas at Ting Ting Art Space
Longtime Colossal readers will likely recognize this cheeky figure as one of Gerard MasвҖҷ brazen busts. For the past few years, the Barcelona-based artist has been taking a playful, contemporary approach to sculpture, casting spirited women in a variety of witty roles.
Rahma Lhoussig, вҖңPlaytime 2вҖқ (2023), mixed technique on canvas, 47 1/5 Г— 59 3/10 inches
6. Rhama Lhoussig at dmincubator
Merging abstraction and magical realism, Rhama Lhoussig paints vivid domestic scenes in which a recurring figure amuses herself with stuffed bears, flowers, toy blocks, and more. Crayon scribbles and crude line drawings fill the surrounding negative space, firmly placing the works in a dynamic moment of creativity.
Dee Clements, вҖңlowers, Vase, BasketsвҖқ (2026), paper, claybord, reed, pine needle, dye, gouache, water-soluble pastel, 31 x 24.5 inches
7. Dee Clements with Mindy Solomon
ChicagoвҖҷs own Dee Clements presents a suite of still-life paintings framed in hand-woven frames, alongside mixed-media sculpture and abstract tapestries.

schema.headline: 7 Artists We're Excited to See at EXPO CHICAGO 2026

title: 50 Years of Chicano Photography
description:
Books Newsletter
                Art books we're reading this spring, a deep dive into Frank O'Hara's curatorial gig at MoMA, and mor
body:
Books Newsletter
                Art books we're reading this spring, a deep dive into Frank O'Hara's curatorial gig at MoMA, and more.
            
                
                                
                            
                
            
        
            I can't stop thinking about this one photo of Dolores Huerta in 1974. She's speaking at a rally onstage, with a sweater vest bearing the iconic United Farmworkers logo вҖ” a white circle with a black thunderbird referencing the Mexican flag.It's one of a trove of photos in Chicano Camera Culture, a must-read on our book list this spring, which happens to include several other titles that explore visual culture and activism. Feminist artist and environmental activist Susan Simensky Bietila has a new memoir out, while tomorrow marks publication day for organizer and painter Molly Crabapple's book on the Jewish Bund (she'll be giving a talk with Naomi Klein at the New York Public Library as the first stop on her book tour). We've also got a Theresa Hak Kyung Cha catalog on our list, marking the first major publication on her work in over two decades.Find more for your spring reading below, including a fun comic by Nathan Gelgud about a book chronicling poet Frank O'Hara's curatorial work at the Museum of Modern Art, and don't forget to let us know what's on your list this season!вҖ”Lakshmi Rivera AminRainy-day reads this month (background image public domain CC0 via the Metropolitan Museum of Art, edit Lakshmi Rivera Amin/Hyperallergic)10 Art Books for Your Spring Reading ListTheresa Hak Kyung ChaвҖҷs first catalog in 25 years, Molly Crabapple chronicles the Jewish Bund, a photographer captures a Black Southern waterway, and more. 
                    
                    
                    
                        вҖңArt doesn't interest me. Artists interest me.вҖқ вҖ”Marcel Duchamp
                        
                            Learn more
                        
                    
            From Our CriticsFrank OвҖҷHaraвҖҷs Curatorial EyeThough best remembered for his poetry, OвҖҷHara championed artists like Helen Frankenthaler and organized several shows at the Museum of Modern Art during the Cold War. | Nathan GelgudFrank OвҖҷHara and MoMA: New York Poet, Global CuratorВ (2025) by Matthew HolmanPreviews & Interviews Joel MeyerowitzвҖҷs photograph of Giorgio MorandiвҖҷs preserved studio in Bologna (В©Joel Meyerowitz; image courtesy Damiani Books)Joel Meyerowitz on Photographing Giorgio MorandiвҖҷs StudioвҖңHe was assembling a force field of geometric objects,вҖқ said Meyerowitz, whose book of images exploring the painterвҖҷs famous still lifes is being rereleased this spring. | Greta Rainbow Nine Lessons on My Path From Engagement to LeadershipMy career has been defined by a steady effort to collapse silos: between curatorial and educational work, between institutions and communities, between what museums have been and what they might yet become. | Ryan N. Dennis
More MeyerowitzJoel Meyerowitz, вҖңSan Cristoabal de las Casas, MexicoвҖқ (1971) (image courtesy Joel Meyerowitz fromВ Wild Flowers, Damiani Books)Joel MeyerowitzвҖҷs Wild Flowers Is a Reminder That the World Is Still BloomingIn its expanded new edition, MeyerowitzвҖҷs photo book makes incidental details the leading characters. | EsmГ© Hogeveen
                  
                      
                        Books Newsletter
                      
                      
                        Newsletter

title: Paul Klee: Other Possible Worlds
description:
Announcement
								The first US museum exhibition to focus on the artistвҖҷs late work, produced in response to the fascism of the 1930s. On
body:
Announcement
								The first US museum exhibition to focus on the artistвҖҷs late work, produced in response to the fascism of the 1930s. On view at the Jewish Museum through July 26, 2026. 

						
								
																
														
								
						
		
	  		    
        
            Paul Klee, "Angel Applicant (Engel-AnwГӨrter)" (1939), opaque watercolor, brush and black ink, and graphite on paper mounted on board, 25 3/4 Г— 17 1/2 inches. The Metropolitan Museum of Art, New York, The Berggruen Klee Collection, 1984, 1984.315.60. (В© 2026 Artists Rights Society (ARS), New York)
    
		
					
						Paul Klee: Other Possible Worlds traces the Swiss-German artistвҖҷs departure from the Bauhaus and his experience throughout the political upheaval of the 1930s prior to his death in 1940. The first US museum exhibition to explore Paul KleeвҖҷs powerful creative output from the final decade of his life, Other Possible Worlds at the Jewish Museum highlights 100 paintings and drawings from across KleeвҖҷs career, including rarely shown works from the 1930s to 1940s. This broader context dramatically frames the artistвҖҷs late practice, during which KleeвҖҷs lifelong individuality and imagination prevail as a form of resistance to Nazi ideology and persecution.Born in 1879 in Switzerland to a music teacher and singer, Klee trained in the violin before shifting to the visual arts during his teenage years. He was involved with a range of burgeoning artistic movements during his early career and went on to establish an esteemed reputation during a decade-long tenure at the Bauhaus. In 1931, seeking to free himself from the demands of lecturing and focus on painting, Klee resigned his position and was offered another at the DГјsseldorf Academy. However, during HitlerвҖҷs ascent to power, the National Socialists deemed KleeвҖҷs art subversive and degenerate and dismissed him from his position, referring to him as вҖңa Galician Jew.вҖқ Forced into exile as an immigrant in his country of birth, Klee abandoned his uplifting chromatic style of painting as he confronted the harsh terrain of fascism and soon, in 1935, the effects of scleroderma, a then-fatal autoimmune disease.Paul Klee, вҖңTropical Blossom (Tropische BlГјte)" (1920), oil and pencil on primed paper on cardboard, 10 1/4 Г— 11 5/16 inches. Zentrum Paul Klee, Bern. (В© 2026 Artists Rights Society (ARS), New York)The exhibition explores this progression, illuminating KleeвҖҷs relentless search for new methods of expressing social critique, non-conformism, mythopoetic thinking, and an evolving approach to developing a new vocabulary for confronting the horrors of political oppression and violence.Curated by Mason Klein, Senior Curator Emeritus, and organized by the Jewish Museum in collaboration with the Zentrum Paul Klee and the Kunstmuseum Bern, Paul Klee: Other Possible Worlds is on view at the Jewish Museum, 5th Ave at 92nd St in New York City, through July 26, 2026.Plan a visit at thejewishmuseum.org. Paul Klee, вҖңUntitled (Last Still Life) (Ohne Titel [Letztes Stilleben])вҖқ (1940), oil on canvas, 39 3/8 Г— 31 11/16 inches. Zentrum Paul Klee, Bern, Livia Klee Donation. (В© 2026 Artists Rights Society (ARS), New York)
									
											
												Announcement
											
											
												Exhibition Announcement
											
											
												Sponsored
											
											
												New York
											
											
												Jewish Museum
